author: Rebecca Walden and Constantine Baecher.
artistdescription: Rebecca Walden and Constantine Baecher are New York-based dancers and choreographers whose work bridges ballet and contemporary expression. Rebecca began her career with Nevada Ballet Theatre and Columbia Classical Ballet, later performing with Armitage Gone! Dance, Terra Firma Dance Theatre, and Ballet Inc. Trained at Pavlovich Dance School, and Southeastern School of Ballet in Columbia, she creates work for New Chamber Ballet and Premiere Division Ballet. A Columbia University graduate, she merges movement with inquiry. Constantine, co-founder of the Copenhagen International Choreography Competition and former Royal Danish Ballet member, has created works worldwide, supported by the Danish Arts Council and Wilhelm Hansen Foundation.

projects.title: One Landscape Artist Residency : Rebecca Walden and Constantine Baecher.

sections.value:
2025 Shillim Institute/One Landscape Artist Residency : Rebecca Walden and Constantine Baecher.

During their residency at Shillim Institute, Constantine Baecher and Rebecca Walden entered into a deeply embodied dialogue with the living landscape of the Western Ghats. Their journey unfolded as a practice of attention, surrender, and discovery—where daily movement training, cultural exchange, and ecological immersion became central to their creative process. Rooted in ballet, modern movement, Gyrotonic practice, and expanded through encounters with Odissi, Marathi folk traditions, and local community participation, their time in Shillim became an evolving study of interconnectedness. Experiences such as village interactions, women’s song circles, workshops with young girls, hikes through forested terrain, visits to Bedse Caves, and contemplative engagements with yoga, meditation, and Vedanta philosophy shaped their artistic sensibility. Rather than imposing choreography upon the land, they allowed the rhythms, textures, and spirit of Shillim to guide them—transforming the residency into a profound exploration of body, place, memory, and shared human expression.

Constantine Baecher and Rebecca Walden’s residency at Shillim Institute blossomed through a rich series of collaborations and site-responsive artistic explorations that drew deeply from the surrounding natural and cultural landscape. Their practice was profoundly enriched through collaboration with fellow resident artists, especially dancer Shatabdi Mallik and musician Jignesh Sheth, resulting in Shillim Trio—a compelling confluence of contemporary movement, ballet, and Odissi, set to an original live-inspired score. This dialogue between international contemporary dance vocabularies and Indian classical form created a nuanced intercultural language, where fluidity, discipline, gesture, and rhythm met in an organically shared space. The artists also engaged meaningfully with the local community through movement and music workshops with village women and young girls, where exchange became reciprocal—classical mudras, ballet foundations, folk dance, stretching, song, and circle movement all intersected as embodied cultural conversation. Across the residency, Shillim’s diverse terrains became both collaborator and catalyst. Works such as Flow Form emerged from the water-shaped rocks of the nallah; Nallah Lizard responded to the heat and basalt textures of the riverbed; Nesting Grass shifted perspective through the imagined world of a field mouse; and Mango Branches explored the body’s relationship with an ancient tree at the House of Confluence. Sky Sculpture drew from the blazing sky and solar energy of the landscape, while Vyoma, inspired by the mountaintop infinity pool, reflected on water, memory, sky, and vastness. Woodland Woman transformed the sight of drying saris into a poetic image of the unravelling of feminine presence in the forest. Their interactive installation, Sari Tree, further extended this relationship with place—drawing from village swings made of sari fabric and found materials. Together, these works formed a sensitive, interdisciplinary body of art where nature, community, and intercultural movement practice converged into a living choreography of Shillim.
